findings.title: GCS_BUCKET_NAME non-null assertion crashes process on missing env var
findings.description: Line 38 uses `process.env.GCS_BUCKET_NAME!` — a TypeScript non-null assertion with no runtime guard. If this env var is absent in a Railway environment (misconfigured deploy, staging misconfiguration, etc.), the `!` does not throw; the value is `undefined`. The subsequent `storage.bucket(undefined)` call will throw at runtime with a cryptic GCS SDK error rather than a clear 'missing environment variable' message. The failure surfaces only when the live-recording download path is triggered, not at startup, so it will not be caught during health checks.
findings.recommendation: Replace with an explicit guard: `const bucketName = process.env.GCS_BUCKET_NAME; if (!bucketName) throw new Error('GCS_BUCKET_NAME environment variable is not set');`

findings.title: yt-dlp subprocess timeout does not kill the child process — hung processes accumulate
findings.description: The `execFileAsync` call sets `timeout: 10 * 60 * 1000` (10 minutes). When Node's `promisify(execFile)` times out, it sends SIGTERM to the child by default. However, if yt-dlp ignores SIGTERM (which it can during certain download phases, and Alpine Linux has a minimal init so zombie cleanup is unreliable), the process stays alive, consuming CPU and holding open file descriptors. Multiple concurrent live-recording jobs would each leave a hung yt-dlp, and the temp directory is only cleaned in the `finally` block — but if the process is still writing to the temp file when cleanup runs, `unlink` on a file being written to can leave partial files or fail silently (errors are swallowed with `.catch(() => {})`). There is no `killSignal: 'SIGKILL'` option set, and no check that the process actually exited.
findings.recommendation: Add `killSignal: 'SIGKILL'` to the execFile options so the OS forcibly terminates a hung yt-dlp. Consider wrapping with `execFile`'s `child.kill()` manually via the non-promisified form if more control is needed. Also add a guard so the finally block waits briefly after timeout before unlinking.

findings.title: Temp directory not cleaned up on GCS upload failure in youtube-video-downloader
findings.description: The `finally` block at lines 105-111 does clean up the temp directory on both success and failure — this part is correct. However, if `readdir(tempDir)` itself throws (permissions error, OS issue), the `.catch(() => [])` returns an empty array, skipping file deletion. Then `rmdir` is called on a non-empty directory, which will throw and be swallowed. The downloaded video file (potentially gigabytes) remains on disk indefinitely. In a container environment with limited ephemeral disk, repeated failures accumulate large orphaned files.
findings.recommendation: Use `rm -rf` equivalent: `fs.rm(tempDir, { recursive: true, force: true })` rather than manually iterating and then rmdiring. This is atomic and handles non-empty directories correctly.

findings.title: Cookie file written to same temp dir — concurrent jobs share no isolation but per-job dirs are safe; real risk is cookie file lingering after process kill
findings.description: Each call to `downloadYouTubeVideoToGcs` creates its own temp dir via `mkdtemp`, so concurrent jobs do not share cookie files — the race condition concern from the review brief does not apply. However, if the process is killed (OOM, SIGKILL from the 30-minute job timeout in processor.ts) between writing the cookies file and the finally block running, the cookie file (containing authentication tokens) is left on disk in `/tmp` until the container is recycled. In a shared-node deployment this is a credential exposure window.
findings.recommendation: Low urgency given Railway's isolated container model, but worth noting. Using `mkdtemp` with `os.tmpdir()` scoped to the container is already reasonable isolation. The bigger gap is the process-kill scenario — document that Railway's ephemeral filesystem mitigates this.

findings.title: 10-second verification delay is insufficient for YouTube thumbnail processing
findings.description: After distribution completes, `processor.ts` waits 10 seconds (`setTimeout(resolve, 10_000)`) then calls `verifyDistribution`. The verification checks YouTube for `maxres` or `high` quality thumbnails to confirm a custom thumbnail was set. YouTube's thumbnail processing pipeline for custom uploads typically takes 30 seconds to several minutes — not 10 seconds. As a result, `verifyYouTube` will almost always flag a thumbnail verification failure immediately after upload, generating spurious alert emails even when the thumbnail eventually propagates correctly. This is a false-positive alarm, not a production outage, but it erodes trust in the alert channel.
findings.recommendation: Increase the delay to at least 60 seconds for the verification wait, or better: make the thumbnail check non-blocking with a separate deferred verification (e.g., schedule a second check 5 minutes later). Alternatively, remove the thumbnail check from immediate post-upload verification since YouTube's processing latency makes it unreliable at any short delay.

findings.title: Verification API calls have no timeout — a slow YouTube/Transistor/WordPress API hangs the verification forever
findings.description: The three `fetch` calls in `verifyYouTube` (line 136), `verifyTransistor` (line 210), and `verifyWordPress` (line 285) have no `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(...)` or equivalent. They run via `Promise.all` in parallel. If any platform API is slow or hangs, the entire verification step blocks indefinitely. The outer `try/catch` in processor.ts (line 742) will not rescue this because `verifyDistribution` itself has no timeout. This holds the job alive past its conceptual completion point and delays any post-processing or resource cleanup.
findings.recommendation: Add `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(15_000)` to each `fetch` call in the verifier. Since these are post-distribution checks and non-fatal, a 15-second per-call timeout is reasonable.

findings.title: Dockerfile downloads yt-dlp and deno from GitHub 'latest' releases — build breaks if release format changes or network is unreliable
findings.description: Lines 38-44 download yt-dlp and deno from GitHub's 'latest' release endpoints at build time with no version pinning, no retry logic, and no checksum verification. Three distinct failure modes: (1) GitHub is unavailable or rate-limits the build runner, causing a hard build failure with no retry; (2) yt-dlp releases a new binary that breaks the expected CLI interface, silently degrading downloads; (3) deno changes its release artifact name or structure (as it has in the past with Linux targets), causing `unzip` to fail. The `wget -q` and `curl -fsSL` flags suppress output, so failures may be harder to diagnose in CI logs. A failed `wget` for yt-dlp will produce a 0-byte or HTML error file at `/usr/local/bin/yt-dlp` that is chmod'd and silently fails at runtime with a confusing error.
findings.recommendation: Pin specific versions (e.g., `yt-dlp/releases/download/2025.03.31/yt-dlp`, `deno/releases/download/v2.x.x/...`). Add `--fail` to wget so a 404 or network error aborts the build immediately. Consider adding SHA256 checksum verification.

findings.title: No timeout on GCS upload after yt-dlp download — large files can stall the job indefinitely
findings.description: After yt-dlp completes, `storage.bucket(bucketName).upload(tempVideoPath, ...)` (line 98) is called with no timeout option. The GCS Node.js SDK does not set a default upload timeout for large files. A multi-gigabyte audio file upload that stalls mid-transfer will hang indefinitely. The outer 30-minute job timeout in `processor.ts` is the only backstop, but by that point the job timeout fires and the GCS upload connection is leaked (no explicit abort). The downloaded temp file is cleaned up by the finally block, but the in-flight GCS connection is not cancelled.
findings.recommendation: Use the GCS SDK's `timeout` option or wrap the upload in an `AbortController` with a reasonable deadline (e.g., 15 minutes for audio upload). Pass the abort signal to the upload call.

findings.title: cache.ts throws on empty WP API response — Next.js unstable_cache may cache the thrown error
findings.description: The `getCachedShows` function throws `new Error("WP API returned no shows — not caching empty result")` when the API returns an empty array (line 9-11), with the intent of preventing caching of transient failures. However, `unstable_cache` in Next.js 15/16 has inconsistent behavior around thrown errors: in some versions it does cache the thrown error (treating it as a settled promise rejection), which means subsequent calls within the revalidation window also throw without hitting the network. The cache key is `["wp-shows"]` — if a transient WP outage triggers the throw and it gets cached, all UI that calls `getCachedShows` will show an error page for up to the full 3600-second revalidation window.
findings.recommendation: Return a sentinel value (e.g., `null` or an empty array with a `stale: true` flag) instead of throwing, and let callers handle the empty case. Alternatively, use `revalidateTag('wp-shows')` from an error boundary to force re-fetch. Throwing inside `unstable_cache` is explicitly called out in Next.js docs as having undefined caching semantics.

findings.title: No timeout on video download from GCS signed URL in processor.ts — fetch can hang indefinitely
findings.description: Line 253 calls `fetch(downloadUrl)` to download the video from a GCS signed URL for YouTube upload. There is no `AbortSignal` or timeout on this fetch. For large video files this download can take many minutes. If GCS is slow or the connection stalls, this blocks indefinitely. The 30-minute outer job timeout is the only guard, but the streaming pipeline (line 258: `pipeline(Readable.fromWeb(...), fileStream)`) also has no timeout. A stalled download consumes the job slot for the full 30 minutes before the timeout fires.
findings.recommendation: Add `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(20 * 60 * 1000)` (or similar) to the `fetch` call for the video download. This allows faster detection of stalled downloads while still accommodating large files.

residual_risks.description: The `issues` array in verify-distribution.ts is mutated via `issues.push(...ytIssues)` from concurrent `Promise.all` callbacks (lines 48, 57, 67). JavaScript's Array.push is not atomic across event loop ticks, but since these are awaited async functions within the map and each pushes after their await completes, there is no actual race condition — JavaScript is single-threaded and the push itself is synchronous. This is safe as written.

yt-dlp writes download progress to stderr on success. Line 85-87 logs stderr as a warning (`console.warn`) even when it contains only normal progress output. This floods logs with warning-level noise but does not affect reliability. It's a log hygiene issue, not a failure mode.

The processor's 30-minute `Promise.race` timeout (processor.ts line 62-69) correctly catches and handles the timeout, marks platforms failed, and sends a notification. The timeout itself fires correctly using `setTimeout` inside a Promise — this is reliable in Node.js server-side context.

testing_gaps.description: No observable test for the yt-dlp timeout path: there is no test that verifies the process is actually killed (SIGKILL) when the 10-minute timeout fires. A test mocking execFile to hang indefinitely would reveal whether zombie processes accumulate.
No test for GCS_BUCKET_NAME being undefined — the non-null assertion means the failure mode is not caught at startup or in tests, only at runtime when a live-recording job is submitted.
No test for the getCachedShows throw path and its interaction with unstable_cache — whether the error is cached or retried on the next request is untested and depends on Next.js internal behavior.
